facts: Canada has more lakes than the rest of the world combined.
It has the longest coastline of any country (202,080 km).
At the Bay of Fundy, the tide can rise as high as a five-story building.
Canada has the world’s only polar bear jail (in Churchill, Manitoba).
The Magnetic Hill in New Brunswick makes your car appear to roll uphill in neutral.
Canada is the second-largest country in the world by total area.
There are parts of the Hudson Bay area that have less gravity than the rest of the planet.
Wood Buffalo National Park is larger than Switzerland.
Canada has six time zones.
Alert, Nunavut, is the northernmost permanently inhabited place in the world.
Canadians eat more Kraft Dinner (Mac & Cheese) than any other nation.
Canada produces about 71% of the world’s maple syrup.
Hawaiian Pizza (pineapple on pizza) was actually invented in Chatham, Ontario.
The Nanaimo Bar originated in Nanaimo, BC, and requires no baking.
Canada has a Global Strategic Maple Syrup Reserve to ensure supply doesn't run dry.
Ketchup-flavored chips are a Canadian staple that are surprisingly hard to find in the US.
The Caesar cocktail was invented in Calgary.
You can buy milk in bags in Ontario and Quebec.
Canadians are the world's largest consumers of doughnuts per capita.
Poutine originated in Quebec in the 1950s; the word is slang for 'mess'.
Basketball was invented by a Canadian, James Naismith.
The Standard Time system was proposed by Sir Sandford Fleming, a Canadian.
Insulin was discovered at the University of Toronto.
The Snowmobile was invented by Joseph-Armand Bombardier.
IMAX was created by three Canadian filmmakers.
The Canadarm is the famous robotic arm used on the Space Shuttle and ISS.
Peanut butter was first patented by a Canadian pharmacist.
The wonderbra was a Canadian invention.
A Canadian patented the first electric light bulb in 1874.
Trivial Pursuit was created by two Canadian journalists.
The 'Apology Act' ensures an apology cannot be used as an admission of guilt in court.
Canada’s official national sports are Lacrosse and Ice Hockey.
There is a UFO landing pad in St. Paul, Alberta, built in 1967.
The official phone number for the Canadian government is 1-800-O-Canada.
The Beaver is the national animal.
In Newfoundland, you might get 'Screeched In,' which involves kissing a frozen cod fish.
Superman was co-created by Canadian Joe Shuster.
In 2012, millions of dollars worth of maple syrup was stolen in the Great Canadian Maple Syrup Heist.
The Loonie ($1 coin) and Toonie ($2 coin) are the nicknames for Canadian currency.
Canada has twice successfully defended invasions from the USA (1775 and 1812).
It is illegal to use too many coins to pay for an item.
Canada has the largest source of fresh water on the planet.
Narcisse, Manitoba, has the highest concentration of snakes in the world every spring.
Residents of Churchill, Manitoba, often leave their car doors unlocked for polar bear escapes.
In the 1940s, Canada had the third-largest navy in the world.
The word 'Canada' comes from the Iroquoian word 'Kanata,' meaning village.
Canada sends a massive Christmas tree to Boston every year to thank them for help after the 1917 Halifax Explosion.
Licensing plates in the Northwest Territories are shaped like Polar Bears.
Canada's literacy rate is 99%.
Canada is the first country in the world to create a national park service.
Winnie the Pooh was named after the city of Winnipeg, Manitoba.
Canada’s official motto is 'A Mari Usque Ad Mare,' which means 'From Sea to Sea.'
The world's smallest jail is located in Rodney, Ontario, and measures only 24.3 square meters.
Canada has the world's largest non-polar ice field in the St. Elias Mountains.
The world's largest Ukrainian Easter Egg (Pysanka) is located in Vegreville, Alberta.
There is a town in Quebec called 'Saint-Louis-du-Ha! Ha!'—the only place in the world with two exclamation marks in its name.
Alberta is the only province in Canada (and one of the few places in the world) that is officially rat-free.
The 1976 Summer Olympics in Montreal were the first Olympics ever held in Canada.
Canada’s oldest company is the Hudson’s Bay Company, incorporated in 1670.
The world's largest totem pole was raised in Alert Bay, British Columbia, standing at 173 feet tall.
The lowest temperature ever recorded in North America was -63°C (-81.4°F) in Snag, Yukon, in 1947.
The West Edmonton Mall was the world’s largest mall until 2004 and still contains the world's largest indoor lake.
The world's largest baseball bat is located in Edmonton, Alberta, and is made of solid steel.
Quebec City is the only fortified city in North America north of Mexico that still has its ramparts.
The town of Vulcan, Alberta, has a Star Trek-themed tourism station and a replica of the Starship Enterprise.
Canada has a 'Snake Highway' in Manitoba where tens of thousands of red-sided garter snakes emerge every spring.
The 'London Fog' (Earl Grey tea latte) was actually invented in Vancouver, British Columbia.
Ogopogo is a famous mythical lake monster said to live in Lake Okanagan, British Columbia.
The Trans-Canada Highway is one of the longest national highways in the world, stretching over 7,800 km.
The world's largest axe is located in Nackawic, New Brunswick, standing 15 meters tall.
Prince Edward Island is the only province with no land border to any other province (until the Confederation Bridge was built).
The world's largest concentration of dinosaur fossils can be found at Dinosaur Provincial Park in Alberta.
Ginger Beef was invented at the Silver Inn in Calgary in the mid-1970s.
The 'Bluenose' schooner, featured on the Canadian dime, was a world-famous racing and fishing vessel.
Canada's first national park was Banff, established in 1885.
The world's largest fiddle is located in Sydney, Nova Scotia, standing 60 feet high.
St. John's, Newfoundland, is the oldest city in North America founded by Europeans.
The 1988 Winter Olympics in Calgary were the first to use artificial snow for all events.
The world's largest paper clip is located in Kipling, Saskatchewan.
Canada has its own version of the Dead Sea, Little Manitou Lake in Saskatchewan, which is five times saltier than the ocean.
The CN Tower in Toronto remained the world’s tallest free-standing structure for 34 years.
The famous red-coated Royal Canadian Mounted Police (RCMP) was originally formed as the North-West Mounted Police.
Montreal is the second-largest French-speaking city in the developed world, after Paris.
The world's largest coin is the 'Big Nickel' in Sudbury, Ontario, which is a giant replica of a 1951 nickel.
Canada’s flag, the Maple Leaf, was only officially adopted on February 15, 1965.
The highest mountain in Canada is Mount Logan in the Yukon, standing at 5,959 meters.
Canada is the world’s largest producer of uranium.
BeaverTails, the iconic Canadian pastry, were first sold at the Killaloe Fair in 1978.
The Yukon Quest is a 1,000-mile dog sled race that is considered one of the toughest in the world.
Moose Jaw, Saskatchewan, is home to 'Mac the Moose,' which was once the world's tallest moose statue.
The world's largest dinosaur statue, a T-Rex, is located in Drumheller, Alberta.
Canada has more caribou than people in the Northwest Territories.
The Great Trail is the world's longest network of recreational multi-use trails, stretching over 24,000 km.
The 'Screech-In' ceremony in Newfoundland involves kissing a cod and taking a shot of local rum.
Ottawa is the seventh coldest capital city in the world.
The world's largest outdoor rodeo is the Calgary Stampede, often called 'The Greatest Outdoor Show on Earth.'
Canada has the world's largest density of wild polar bears in the Hudson Bay region.
The world's largest lobster statue is located in Shediac, New Brunswick.
Canada is the only country to have hosted the Olympics three times and not win a gold medal at two of them (1976 and 1988).
The world's largest stash of emeralds in North America was found in the Yukon in 2003.
